does: This paper evaluates small language models as governance validators in decentralized autonomous organizations by conducting controlled ablation studies on Qwen-3.5-9B using a 840-inference benchmark dataset derived from Optimism DAO proposals. The authors systematically disable and enable inference-time reasoning (System 2) while keeping model weights frozen, measuring performance across adversarial robustness, juridical consistency, and latency. Their central finding challenges conventional wisdom: the simpler autoregressive baseline (System 1) achieved perfect adversarial robustness and juridical consistency with 13-second latency, while chain-of-thought reasoning (System 2) introduced severe instability with 26.7% reasoning non-convergence rates, degraded consensus stability to 72.6%, and imposed 17x latency penalties. The paper demonstrates that inference-time compute scaling creates a catastrophic performance inversion in cryptoeconomic governance contexts.
connects: This work extends research on deploying language models in high-stakes, adversarial settings by combining insights from reasoning scaling literature with practical constraints of decentralized governance systems. It builds on prior work examining whether larger inference-time compute budgets improve model reliability, a debate prominent in interpretability and constitutional AI research. The paper specifically responds to the assumption that System 2 reasoning enhances robustness in formal verification tasks, drawing on Kahneman's dual-process framework applied to AI systems. It connects to governance-focused AI research exploring how language models can serve as gatekeepers against social engineering, and relates to empirical studies on consensus mechanisms and Byzantine fault tolerance in distributed systems. The work also engages with growing literature on SLM efficiency in resource-constrained edge deployments.
enables: These findings suggest a fundamentally different approach to deploying language models in adversarial governance contexts, potentially shifting deployment strategies from compute-intensive reasoning systems toward lean, deterministic baselines optimized for consistency. The work points toward future research examining why inference-time compute destabilizes performance in governance domains specifically, opening investigation into reasoning-stability trade-offs. It enables development of reasoning verification methods that can predict convergence failures before deployment. The results support building DAO infrastructure around lightweight SLM validators with rigorous formal guarantees rather than complex reasoning chains, and motivate research into hybrid approaches combining System 1 speed with selective System 2 reasoning for specific governance scenarios. Future work could explore whether the cognitive collapse phenomenon extends to other high-stakes domains or reflects governance-specific adversarial structures.
